data.title: Blue Owl stock closes at record low as private credit investors head for exits
data.description:
Blue Owl Capital Inc. (OWL) stock dropped 1.4% to $8.45 on Monday, bringing it to a record low closing price of $8.44 as concerns over the $1.8T  private credit market fuel rising redemption requests.

Business development companies, which directly lend to small- and middle-market companies, bore the brunt of the trepidation as they typically have heavier exposure to software companies than other lenders, such as banks. Legacy software stocks came under pressure on fears that agentic artificial intelligence would make large swaths of the industry obsolete.

Blue Owl (OWL) started publicly trading in May 2021 when a special purpose acquisition company, or SPAC, merged Owl Rock Capital Group with Dyal Capital Partners. In the past five years, its stock declined 14%, a stark contrast to the S&P 500's 64% increase during the same period. The decline gathered pace in recent months, with Blue Owl dropping 43% since the start of the year.

Altimar Acquisition, the SPAC that acquired the two BDC companies, went public at $10 per share.

Blue Owl (OWL) has come under severe pressure due to its elevated exposure to software firms. One of its technology-focused funds, OCIC, received redemption requests exceeding 20% of its shares outstanding, far more than its 5% limit on share redemptions. 

The lack of confidence in the stock also shows up in shorting activity. It's one of the most shorted stocks, with short interest of about 18.84% at the end of March. Short selling is a way for investors to profit when a stock falls.

In after-hours trading, Blue Owl (OWL) stock edged up 0.3%.

data.title: Neuronetics CFO Steven Pfanstiel to depart; company begins search for successor
data.description:
• Neuronetics (STIM) the maker of NeuroStar Advanced Therapy, on Monday said that Executive Vice President, Chief Financial Officer, and Treasurer Steven E. Pfanstiel will leave the company to pursue an opportunity outside the organization.
  • Pfanstiel will remain through May 1, 2026, to ensure a smooth transition.
  • The company confirmed his departure is not due to any disputes or disagreements with management or the Board and has initiated a search for a new CFO.
  • STIM +13.63% after hours to $2.0.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Markets are pricing in normalization from the Iran war; upcoming economic data may paint a clearer picture
data.description:
Wall Street is pricing in a successful end of the war in a few weeks, the normalization of oil supplies over the summer, and unchanged interest rates throughout the year, according to Seeking Alpha analyst Damir Tokic.

However, U.S. President Donald Trump has continued to escalate pressure on Tehran, threatening to target power plants, bridges, and other critical facilities if the waterway remains closed. The Iranian regime, on the other hand, has rejected every ceasefire effort.

“As Tuesday's deadline for Iran to open Hormuz approaches, financial markets remain stable, pricing a successful end of the war within the next 2-3 weeks. The oil futures (CO1:COM), (CL1:COM) curve is pricing a gradual normalization of oil supplies over the summer. The Fed is expected to keep the rates unchanged through 2026, while the stock market (SP500), (DJI), (COMP:IND) bounce continues,” Tokic said.

“However, the subjective probability of a ceasefire is very low, as Iran rejected the ceasefire proposal,” he continued. “Thus, volatility is likely to return as the situation unfolds, which is also signaled by a rising VIX (VIX) despite the bounce in the S&P 500 (SP500).”

Joseph Brusuelas, RSM U.S. principal and chief economist, added that commodities exported through the Strait of Hormuz matter to the American economy.

“The U.S. economy has absorbed the shock caused by the first month of the war in relatively good shape,” he said. “Both the ISM manufacturing and services surveys imply modest to solid expansion through the first month of the war.”

“We will get a better view of the cost to the American public this week via the March U.S. CPI report, where we expect a 1% jump in inflation on the month, which should translate to a 3.5% year-ago increase with risk of a greater increase,” he added.

More on the U.S. economy

  • Dollar eases as geopolitical uncertainty keeps markets cautious: Currency Recap
  • ISM Services PMI growth slows more than expected in March
  • Top global stories this week includes TSM, Unilever
  • Trump threatens strikes on Iranian infrastructure as conflict escalates
  • U.S. Composite PMI falls more than expected in March

data.title: American Integrity Insurance Group appoints Brian Foley as CFO
data.description:
• American Integrity Insurance Group (AII) announced on Monday the appointment of Brian Foley as chief financial officer, effective April 6, 2026.
  • Most recently, Foley served as an investment banker at Keefe, Bruyette & Woods, A Stifel Company (“KBW”), where he advised insurance companies on a broad range of capital raising and strategic transactions. Earlier in his career, Foley was an equity analyst at Balyasny Asset Management. 
  • Foley succeeds Ben Lurie, who will transition to a consulting role and continue to serve on the board of directors of the company’s insurance subsidiary, as well as co-chairman of its investment committee. Lurie is returning to his role as chief financial officer of Sowell & Co., the founding investor of American Integrity.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Morgan Stanley plans interval fund focused predominantly on private credit
data.description:
Morgan Stanley (MS) plans to launch the North Haven Strategic Credit Fund, a non-diversified, closed-end management investment company that operates as an interval fund.

The interval fund is expected to offer single access to a wide spectrum of credit strategies, including private corporate loans, private hybrid financing solutions, private asset-based loans, private asset-backed lending facilities, broadly syndicated loans, high-yield bonds, securitized assets, and emerging market debt.

As a fundamental policy, it will make quarterly offers to repurchase between 5% and 25% of its outstanding shares at net asset value. However, the general anticipation is making repurchases of 5% of outstanding shares on a quarterly basis.

The news comes amid growing concerns about systemic risks in the $1.8T private credit market.

data.title: Roblox investors should brace for soft Q2 guidance—Wells Fargo
data.description:
In what is likely to be a “challenging waiting game for investors,” Wells Fargo’s Ken Gawrelski sees growth metrics for Roblox (RBLX) to become “meaningfully tougher” each month of the second quarter, translating into expectations for softer guidance for Q2.

While the longer-term outlook for the company is bullish, Gawrelski expects Q2 bookings guidance of $1.765B to $1.80B (representing 23% to 25% year-over-year growth) will be below the consensus estimates of $1.875B (30% growth). Meanwhile, profitability challenges will be reflected in negative EBITDA and a gross profit margin of 24%.

“We still believe FY26 guidance is achievable but will be more [second half of 2026] dependent,” Gawrelski says.

To reflect the 54% retracement in Roblox (RBLX) over the past 6 months, Gawrelski lowered his price target by 20% to $78 but maintained an Overweight rating as the company’s expansion into the ad business “represents a large opportunity.”

Moreover, a higher incremental margin for the ad business (versus microtransactions) will also allow Roblox (RBLX) to expand its margin at a faster pace than the current target of 100-300 basis points.

Shares ended the day 5% lower to reflect the likelihood of lackluster guidance when the company reports first-quarter results before the market open on April 30, at which time Roblox (RBLX) is expected to report an adjusted loss of $0.41 per share on $1.75B in revenue.

data.title: Sable Offshore slides as California AG opposes request to waive federal safety rule
data.description:
Sable Offshore (SOC) closed down 7.9% in Monday's trading after California Attorney General Rob Bonta officially announced the state's opposition to the U.S. Pipeline and Hazardous Materials Safety Administration's consideration of the company's request to waive a safety regulation and transport oil through Santa Barbara and Kern counties.

PHMSA is without jurisdiction to issue any special permit because Sable's (SOC) pipelines are intrastate and therefore subject to state and not federal regulation, Bonta argued in the comment letter in opposition to the company's request.

The agency "illegally" reclassified the pipelines that run from Santa Barbara County to Kern County as "interstate," after previously classifying the pipelines as "intrastate" pipelines subject to state safety regulation and oversight, the AG said.

"Let's be clear: We do not have a 'National Energy Emergency,'" Bonta wrote. "President Trump is simply prioritizing the fossil fuel industry, ensuring that they can continue to line their own pockets by illegally restarting oil transportation through California’s pipelines at the expense of our public health and environment."

data.title: Cisco names former Deloitte executive Shimer to board
data.description:
• Cisco Systems (CSCO) said on Monday that it has named former Deloitte executive  Peter Shimer to its board of directors.
  • Shimer, who has four decades worth of executive experience, including as a member of the C-suite, will be a part of the board's audit committee, Cisco said in a statement. 
  • “We're delighted to welcome Pete to Cisco's board,” said Chuck Robbins, chair and CEO of Cisco. “His four decades of experience leading large global organizations and guiding clients through digital transformation brings invaluable insight at a critical moment, as Cisco delivers the critical infrastructure powering AI innovation worldwide.”
  • Shimer is also a member of the board of directors at Alaska Airlines, Korn Ferry, and Synopsys (SNPS), and is the executive chair of the Cancer Artificial Intelligence Alliance.

data.title: Wall Street ends higher as traders price in de-escalation, while Iran rejects ceasefire proposal
data.description:
U.S. stocks finished higher on Monday as traders remained focused on developments in the Middle East, including the status of the Strait of Hormuz.

The tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) closed +0.5%, while the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) ended +0.5%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) finished +0.4%. 

“As the Tuesday's deadline to Iran to open Hormuz approaches, financial markets remain stable pricing a successful end of the war within the next 2-3 weeks,” said Seeking Alpha analyst Damir Tokic.

“The oil futures curve is pricing a gradual normalization of oil supplies over the summer, the Fed is expected to keep the rates unchanged thought 2026, while the stock market bounce continues,” Tokic added.

“However, the subjective probability of ceasefire is very low, as Iran rejected the ceasefire proposal. Thus, volatility is likely return as the situation unfolds, which is also signaled by a rising VIX (VIX) despite the bounce in the S&P 500 (SP500),” he concluded.

On a sector-by-sector basis, eight of the 11 S&P segments were in the green, with consumer discretionary at the top. At the other end of the spectrum, utilities was the weakest performer. 

President Donald Trump has given Iran until 8 p.m. Eastern time Tuesday to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, warning that failure to comply could trigger U.S. attacks on key infrastructure across the country.

The U.S., Iran, and regional mediators are negotiating a potential 45-day ceasefire that could pave the way for ending the war, Axios reported. 

U.S. Treasury yields climbed across the curve. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose 2 basis points to 4.34%, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) moved up 3 basis points to 3.86%. The 30-year yield (US30Y) edged lower by 2 basis points to 4.90%.

On the economic front, March U.S. ISM Services PMI Index data arrived at 54.0 versus the. 54.7 consensus and 56.1 in February figures.

As for stocks that were on the move, Seagate Technology (STX) climbed +5.5%, while shares of Datadog (DDOG) dropped -3.2%.

data.title: SpaceX's IPO valuation is said to hinge on Elon Musk's vision
data.description:
SpaceX (SPACE) and its bankers are preparing investor meetings to gauge demand for a potential IPO that could value the company at around $2 trillion, Bloomberg News reported Monday, citing people familiar with the plans.

The discussions come shortly after SpaceX’s merger with xAI, as the company explores what could become the largest IPO on record. Estimates for its valuation have climbed quickly, rising from about $1.75 trillion to above $2 trillion in recent weeks.

Some observers say the pitch to investors will hinge less on current financials and more on belief in Elon Musk’s long-term vision. One academic and former banker noted that the focus is likely to remain on sustaining investor enthusiasm for the company’s future potential rather than strictly justifying the numbers today.

At that valuation, SpaceX (SPACE) would rank among the most valuable companies in the S&P 500, despite generating far less revenue than many of its peers. Analyst estimates suggest the company could produce close to $20 billion in revenue by 2026, largely from its launch business and Starlink satellite network.

The IPO timeline may depend in part on upcoming rocket test launches. Analysts say successful tests could build confidence, while setbacks might delay or even derail the offering.

A listing could raise as much as $75 billion, surpassing the record set by Saudi Aramco in 2019. The proceeds would help fund ambitious projects, including space-based data infrastructure and other long-term initiatives tied to Musk’s broader technology ecosystem.

Still, some analysts caution that as the valuation rises, investors may question how much of the story rests on Musk’s ability to inspire confidence rather than on underlying financial performance, Bloomberg News reported.
